lines.text: "A toddler playing with a loaded gun because the chrome is so shiny.". And if that doesn't tell you exactly what kind of story we're walking into, I don't know what will.

God, that narrator. It's watching Oliver from somewhere in the future, and there's this heartbreak in it, right? Like they already know how badly this is going to go. "It's the kind of naivety that makes me want to reach through the glass and scream."

What's brilliant is the structure here. We get this gorgeous, almost mythological fleet scene—the Yggdrasil, the Freya, all these Norse names—and then the narrator literally says, "we're done with the fairy tales." Look at what they built this colony on—the text says "millions waived their humanitarian rights." Corruption in supply chains. Old demons coming back stronger. The rot might have been there from the start. And then we snap forward to nutripaste and rust and a kid scraping gel off turbines.

The contrast is brutal. Young Oliver watching ships that move like "continents drifting," his mother pressing her forehead to his, that fever-warm skin. And then adult Oliver waking up to synthetic tangerine scent and sonic tooth-cleaning bots. Even his dental health goes into some database.
Back to the Grid. It regulates food, energy, medicine, and now moods. Ian says he can't remember the last time he saw a real smile.

And Joana's research confirms something else is going on. She finds this pattern in the EEG data—everyone with symptoms was among the first landing crews. The ones who breathed Mairee's unfiltered air. There's something in this planet. Something that's getting into people's heads. Her daughter Su's affected strongly somehow.

It's getting to Ian, the man installing the Grid. This exhausted man who feels like a "marionette creating the master of puppets," and he finds this kid playing an illegal holoviser game. And instead of reporting him, he helps. He makes the bypass better.
"Scrapper vs Juggernaut.". Oliver's quick, adaptive little mech dancing around Ian's lumbering brawler. It's a metaphor to something else that's coming, isn't it?
Oliver's actions are a microcosm of the larger struggle for change.

And then later, after scraping those turbines, when Oliver passes the Rec-Zone, sees kids his age laughing, and he won't let himself join them. "Their laughter hurt him." He's already separate. That's how trauma manifests. He's not a kid anymore.
